full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> There's a movement afoot in some parishes in the Episcopal Church to change up the liturgy a little on Palm Sunday,<|9.44|> <|9.44|> and to focus on the triumphal entry of Jesus in Jerusalem, and to omit the reading of the Passion,<|17.76|> <|18.64|> leaving it only to be read on Good Friday.<|20.70|> <|20.70|> Amen.<|21.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Part of the argument in favor of this change is that there is simply too much going on in the Palm Sunday liturgy, and it's hard to know what to focus on, especially since we experience the depth of the Passion on Good Friday.<|18.28|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The counter argument, however, against ignoring the Passion reading on Palm Sunday is that<|7.36|> <|7.36|> many people do not attend the services of Holy Week, and it would be both a theological<|14.04|> <|14.04|> and liturgical problem never to have the Passion story read on a Sunday, especially not the<|21.52|> <|21.52|> Sunday before Easter.<|24.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Both sides have good cases, but one of the things I believe Palm Sunday does better than any other liturgical day of the year is to present to us with both clarity and severity not only the extraordinary power that crowds can exert over political authorities,<|24.00|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> but how utterly fickle, self-interested, and how fast we can turn on someone who doesn't live up to our expectations.<|10.36|> <|12.54|> Hailed as a king one day and betrayed by his own people and killed on a cross five days later,<|20.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We need this spiritual and emotional whiplash to remind us of our own failures in discipleship,<|8.28|> <|8.94|> to be reminded about how fickle, cruel, and self-interested we can be in our own relationship to Christ, the church,<|19.94|> <|20.56|> and to those we live with each and every day.<|24.42|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.18|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I witnessed this firsthand once when a large portion of a congregation turned on a pastor of a church.<|10.00|> <|10.80|> A pastor who had faithfully served the congregation for 25 years and who was much beloved by everyone.<|19.74|> <|19.74|> Amen.<|20.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But then one day he made a controversial decision.<|5.70|> <|5.70|> The decision was in the best interest of the church,<|9.46|> <|9.46|> both in the near and the long term,<|12.40|> <|12.40|> and was supported by the vestry with an 18 to 3 vote.<|18.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But those who disagreed with the pastor left the church.<|4.74|> <|6.08|> They withheld their pledges, and they wrote vitriolic and truly vile notes to him.<|12.70|> <|14.12|> These were people who, just a week before, had had the pastor over to their house for dinner,<|19.72|> <|20.66|> who over the years had their children baptized, confirmed, and married by him.<|26.80|> <|26.80|> Amen.<|27.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It was astonishing to witness.<|4.00|> <|4.00|> I thought, perhaps naively,<|8.10|> <|8.10|> that 25 years of personal relationships,<|12.50|> <|12.50|> being with people through joys and sorrows and the crisis of life,<|18.50|> <|18.50|> being a steady and good shepherd to the faithful,<|22.50|> <|22.50|> would have provided some sort of grace,<|25.50|> <|25.50|> some sort of benefit of the doubt.<|28.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Yet it didn't.<|3.00|> <|3.00|> The relationships dissolved just like that.<|8.00|> <|8.00|> By every description, those who rejected the pastor were good, faithful Christians.<|16.00|> <|16.00|> They went to church regularly.<|19.00|> <|19.00|> They volunteered.<|21.00|> <|21.00|> They attended formation classes.<|23.00|> <|23.00|> They were generous pledgers.<|26.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But all that was gone in one instant with one decision.<|5.52|> <|6.64|> And it was a decision that the pastor made that no longer represented their cultural, political, or ideological worldviews.<|19.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The pastor had not committed heresy,<|3.54|> <|3.54|> he had not violated a single canon,<|6.40|> <|6.40|> he had not blasphemed God,<|8.84|> <|8.84|> he hadn't changed the liturgy,<|11.18|> <|11.18|> he didn't preach a bad sermon,<|13.52|> <|13.52|> and he hadn't committed any crime.<|16.42|> <|16.42|> But he simply made a decision which reflected the spirit of<|20.32|> <|20.32|> the gospel in a highly politically charged environment.<|25.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And this incited people to abandon their church home of decades, reject a once beloved pastor, and walk out the door, leaving those who remained in grief and wondering how they were going to rebuild the church.<|18.46|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> While this story is not nearly on the scale and significance of the crowds turning on Jesus in the passage we read today,<|9.42|> <|10.04|> the phenomenon is similar.<|12.46|> <|13.68|> With my clergy colleague, it was, we love you, one day, to we hate you, the next.<|20.96|> <|22.56|> Perhaps some of you have even experienced this yourselves.<|26.72|> <|26.72|> Perhaps some of you have experienced this.<|27.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> With Jesus, it was Hosanna, save us, to five days later, give us Barabbas, crucify him.<|9.08|> <|11.22|> As we know, the Jews at the time of Christ were under the rule of the Roman Empire.<|16.12|> <|17.12|> While the Jews weren't slaves and were free to practice their religious beliefs,<|22.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> the Jews were often excessively taxed.<|3.70|> <|3.70|> And the Roman legal system, as well as Rome's cultural and religious practices,<|9.80|> <|9.80|> often caused great tensions between the Jews and the Romans.<|15.30|> <|15.30|> There was also political stability among Rome as well.<|20.70|> <|20.70|> And the Romans were divided into factions,<|23.80|> <|23.80|> which further exacerbated the political situation in Judea.<|29.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.66|> So what did the Jews want exactly?<|4.20|> <|5.40|> They wanted a Messiah, but they wanted a Messiah like the way Moses defeated Pharaoh<|11.54|> <|12.18|> and delivered the Israelites from Egypt.<|14.90|> <|16.34|> The Jews wanted a Messiah to deliver them from Rome.<|21.34|> <|23.22|> They wanted a political revolutionary.<|25.56|> <|26.66|> They did not want a redeemer.<|28.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And they thought Jesus was that person.<|4.36|> <|5.56|> His actions were fulfilling all the prophecies.<|8.58|> <|9.22|> He raised Lazarus from the dead just days before.<|12.18|> <|12.90|> He fed thousands in the wilderness.<|14.84|> <|15.66|> He healed the sick.<|16.70|> <|17.08|> He gave sight to the blind.<|18.44|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The Jews now had in their midst a man who had shown power they had never seen before,<|6.78|> <|7.52|> and they believed Jesus was the king they had been waiting for.<|12.48|> <|14.28|> But if they had been paying close attention,<|16.86|> <|17.62|> they would remember that Jesus never said he would deliver them from Rome.<|23.84|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> He said he came to deliver people from their sins.<|5.46|> <|6.74|> Time and time again, Jesus said,<|9.22|> <|9.60|> my kingdom is not of this world.<|12.24|> <|13.42|> Jesus did not allow his disciples to fight on his behalf.<|17.98|> <|19.14|> He offered parables saying,<|20.92|> <|21.42|> the kingdom of heaven is like a mustard seed.<|23.82|> <|23.82|> The kingdom of heaven is like a field of laborers.<|28.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But obviously no one, not even Jesus's closest disciples, understood exactly what he meant.<|10.34|> <|12.46|> Like most people throughout human history, they wanted a political leader.<|18.66|> <|18.66|> They wanted a political leader.<|18.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.00|> And not only a political leader, they wanted a political leader who was like them.<|7.00|> <|7.60|> They wanted a political environment that was consistent with their own cultural,<|12.80|> <|12.80|> religious and political beliefs.<|16.80|> <|17.16|> They longed for the days of Israel under King David.<|21.80|> <|22.20|> And with Jesus, they thought that that day was at hand.<|27.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And so here we are on Palm Sunday.<|5.00|> <|5.00|> Jesus doesn't enter into Jerusalem on a war horse,<|9.00|> <|9.00|> which would have been a symbol of military and political power.<|15.00|> <|15.00|> But he enters on a donkey, a sign of lowliness and of humility,<|22.00|> <|22.00|> fulfilling the prophecy of Isaiah.<|25.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And after Jesus is betrayed and arrested and stands toe to toe with the political rulers<|7.66|> <|7.66|> and the power structure of his day, what did he do?<|13.66|> <|13.66|> He said very little.<|16.46|> <|16.46|> He gave no long speeches.<|18.88|> <|18.88|> He didn't make any arguments.<|21.44|> <|21.44|> He didn't shame the Romans.<|23.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> There was no spout of righteous indignation, no plea for his life, and no demonstration of power.<|8.92|> <|10.44|> But his conviction about his purpose was on full display in front of Pilate, and that was his true power.<|20.46|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.14|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But this is not how the Jews expected Jesus to act in front of Pilate.<|5.22|> <|6.40|> So when they saw that Jesus was going to surrender himself and not use any of his power against Pilate,<|13.70|> <|14.64|> that's when the crowds turned on him swiftly and decisively.<|20.38|> <|20.38|> Amen.<|21.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> They didn't want a humble, meek son of God to deliver them from their sins.<|7.04|> <|7.46|> They wanted an omnipotent, all-powerful ruler to bring Rome and any other political enemy to heal.<|17.10|> <|17.10|> Amen.<|18.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But like all people who look to political rulers to save them and to usher in their vision of utopia, their vision is short-sighted and they are inevitably disappointed.<|14.06|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So even after Pilate declares Jesus innocent not once, not twice, but three times, the crowds shout to crucify him.<|10.72|> <|12.00|> And who did they choose to release instead of Jesus?<|15.40|> <|16.32|> They chose Barabbas.<|18.20|> <|19.50|> But why?<|20.82|> <|20.82|> Why?<|21.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.08|> Because Barabbas was a rebel.<|4.24|> <|5.16|> He was an insurrectionist.<|7.88|> <|7.88|> He was a murderer.<|10.24|> <|10.24|> And he was the kind of person who was trying to overthrow Rome.<|14.60|> <|16.72|> Barabbas was the man who symbolized the kind of power<|20.12|> <|20.48|> the Jews wanted among them and to fight on their behalf.<|26.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> My charge to all of us in the week ahead is to interrogate ourselves very closely.<|9.50|> <|9.50|> If Jesus came back today and didn't stand up to political leaders around the globe the way we would want him to, what would we do?<|22.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> If Jesus came back today and reminded us that the kingdom of heaven is entered into through faithful suffering, obedience, and death, and not by political and military power, what would we do?<|17.98|> <|17.98|> What would we do?<|19.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> If Jesus came back today and was seized by the authorities, and we could have any prisoner<|8.82|> <|8.82|> returned to us, who would we choose in Jesus' place?<|14.58|> <|16.16|> Our honest answers to these questions should lead us to repentance and should give shape<|23.36|> <|23.36|> to our future prayers.<|24.92|> <|24.92|> Amen.<|26.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The spiritual and emotional whiplash of Palm Sunday reminds us of the folly and danger of expecting Jesus to be someone he isn't.<|13.98|> <|15.88|> Palm Sunday reminds us of our own failed discipleship.<|19.82|> <|19.82|> When we create Jesus in our own image as the earthly political leader that we want.<|28.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.22|> Palm Sunday reminds us that we are the crowds in the story.<|4.60|> <|5.64|> It reminds us of our own fickleness of faith<|8.32|> <|8.32|> when the Jesus of Holy Scriptures does not live up to our expectations.<|13.94|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> questions.<|1.96|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Yet lest we despair, we can look to Jesus on the cross and see that with his last breath, he still speaks of forgiveness, grace, and mercy.<|14.42|> <|16.06|> May God have mercy on us all. Amen.<|19.74|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|0.10|><|endoftext|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.08|> Because Barabbas was a rebel.<|4.24|>
<|5.16|> He was an insurrectionist.<|7.88|>
<|7.88|> He was a murderer.<|10.24|>
<|10.24|> And he was the kind of person who was trying to overthrow Rome.<|14.60|>
<|16.72|> Barabbas was the man who symbolized the kind of power<|20.12|>
<|20.48|> the Jews wanted among them and to fight on their behalf.<|26.60|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> My charge to all of us in the week ahead is to interrogate ourselves very closely.<|9.50|>
<|9.50|> If Jesus came back today and didn't stand up to political leaders around the globe the way we would want him to, what would we do?<|22.80|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> If Jesus came back today and reminded us that the kingdom of heaven is entered into through faithful suffering, obedience, and death, and not by political and military power, what would we do?<|17.98|>
<|17.98|> What would we do?<|19.00|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> If Jesus came back today and was seized by the authorities, and we could have any prisoner<|8.82|>
<|8.82|> returned to us, who would we choose in Jesus' place?<|14.58|>
<|16.16|> Our honest answers to these questions should lead us to repentance and should give shape<|23.36|>
<|23.36|> to our future prayers.<|24.92|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The spiritual and emotional whiplash of Palm Sunday reminds us of the folly and danger of expecting Jesus to be someone he isn't.<|13.98|>
<|15.88|> Palm Sunday reminds us of our own failed discipleship.<|19.82|>
<|19.82|> When we create Jesus in our own image as the earthly political leader that we want.<|28.80|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.22|> Palm Sunday reminds us that we are the crowds in the story.<|4.60|>
<|5.64|> It reminds us of our own fickleness of faith<|8.32|>
<|8.32|> when the Jesus of Holy Scriptures does not live up to our expectations.<|13.94|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> questions.<|1.96|><|endoftext|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Yet lest we despair, we can look to Jesus on the cross and see that with his last breath, he still speaks of forgiveness, grace, and mercy.<|14.42|>
<|16.06|> May God have mercy on us all. Amen.<|19.74|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|0.10|><|endoftext|>
